title: SEGMENT 15 — Fish CRISPR and the fastest vertebrate genetics

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Fish CRISPR and the fastest vertebrate genetics
Forward genetics gave the field its first generation of fish mutants.

Reverse genetics — picking a gene first, knocking it out, and asking what happens — was much slower to arrive, because the homologous-recombination techniques that worked in mouse ES cells didn't work in fish.

For about fifteen years, fish reverse genetics relied on awkward workarounds: morpholinos (modified antisense oligos that block mRNA processing or translation, but produce off-target effects and don't make heritable mutants) and TILLING (looking through large mutagenized libraries for random point mutations in your gene of interest, then breeding them out).
Then, around 2013, CRISPR-Cas9 arrived in fish, and fish reverse genetics suddenly became, in some ways, the fastest reverse genetics of any vertebrate.
Here's why fish are exceptionally well-suited to CRISPR.
One: injection is routine.
Fish embryos at the one-cell stage are sitting in a dish of water, externally fertilized, easily accessible to a micropipette.
You can inject CRISPR reagents — a Cas9 protein or mRNA plus a guide RNA — into hundreds of embryos in a morning.
In mouse, injection requires hormonal superovulation, embryo collection by dissection from sacrificed females, and reimplantation into pseudopregnant foster mothers.
In fish, it's just water.
Two: F0 phenotyping.

Because zebrafish are diploid and a fresh CRISPR injection produces mosaic mutations across the embryo's cells, you can often see a phenotype in the founder generation itself — the F0, the directly injected embryos.
If Cas9 cuts the gene in enough cells, the resulting tissue is mostly mutant, and any phenotype shows up immediately.
You don't have to breed for two generations to see effects, the way you do in mouse.
Three: speed.
From "I want to knock out gene X" to "I am holding embryos with gene X knocked out" can take a couple of weeks in fish.
From "I want to knock out gene X" to "I have homozygous knockout mouse embryos for studying" takes several months even with CRISPR streamlining.
The fish system is just faster.
Four: scale.
You can run a CRISPR-based reverse-genetic screen across hundreds of genes in fish, in a year, in one lab.
The equivalent screen in mouse would take decades and cost millions.
The result is that zebrafish, in 2026, is the fastest vertebrate genetics platform available.

If you have a candidate gene from a human GWAS — a gene you suspect contributes to a human disease — you can clone the fish ortholog, knock it out with CRISPR, and have phenotype data in a vertebrate within a month.
That kind of cycle time is changing how human disease genetics gets validated.
Every time a new candidate gene comes out of a clinical sequencing project, somebody asks: what does it do in fish?
The big idea: CRISPR turned zebrafish from a screen-driven forward-genetic system into a hypothesis-driven reverse-genetic system, while keeping all the fish's advantages.
Same fast generation time, same transparent embryos, same ease of injection — now with the ability to target any gene on demand.
Fish reverse genetics is, in some sense, the natural home for rapid functional validation of human disease genes.
